summary: Gruber analyzes David Leonhardt's NYT column identifying Facebook's four core problems (age, innovation, metaverse, antitrust), noting approvingly that Apple's App Tracking Transparency didn't even make the list. He argues Zuckerberg has been using ATT as a distraction from deeper systemic issues. Gruber reframes Meta's VR bet not as a belief that VR is the future, but as Zuckerberg's desperate need to own a platform after missing mobile. He compares Facebook's trajectory to Yahoo's decline and suggests the Meta rebrand was less about metaverse commitment than escaping a poisoned brand name.
key_points: ATT is not among Facebook's top problems — all four of Leonhardt's identified problems would exist even without it, and Zuckerberg uses ATT complaints to distract from deeper issues
Meta's VR bet is really about platform ownership: Facebook missed mobile, AR technology isn't ready yet, so VR is the only available option for controlling a platform
The Meta rebrand parallels Philip Morris changing to Altria — it's about escaping a toxic brand, not proving metaverse commitment
Facebook may be entering a Yahoo-like long slow decline into irrelevance
VR vs. social media is the wrong comparison — they're different levels of the stack, like owning a grocery chain vs. growing fruit
money_quotes: I look forward to dancing on Facebook's grave if, say, Verizon or AT&T acquires them after a stock collapse.

I think Mark Zuckerberg knows this better than anyone, and he's been trying to focus public attention on ATT as an unfair attack by Apple, a competitor, to distract from Facebook's more serious systemic problems.
Zuckerberg just wants to own a platform, it's far too late for that to be a phone platform, AR technology isn't here yet, so ... VR it is.
I also think they changed the name of the holding company from 'Facebook' to 'Meta' not as proof of that commitment but because the name Facebook is now poison in the public mind.
It's not even apples-vs.-oranges because apples and oranges are both fruit. It's more like owning a grocery store chain vs. growing fruit to sell in grocery stores.
The social networks play in Apple and Google's gardens.
I'm not counting them out, but unless something dramatic changes, I think we've seen the beginning of a long slow decline into general irrelevance.
key_insight: Facebook's VR pivot isn't really about believing in the metaverse — it's a belated, desperate attempt to own a platform after the company missed mobile and found itself at the mercy of Apple and Google.
